Q1 — Place: There's a stairwell in the apartment building I grew up in — concrete, no carpeting. The acoustics did this strange thing where if you hummed at a particular pitch, the stairwell hummed back at a slightly different one. I used to sit on the third-floor landing and just experiment with which notes set the building ringing and which ones it absorbed. I never told anyone. I'd think of it as the building's voice. Some pitches it loved and some made it go dead silent. I still pass by stairwells now and want to find out what they sound like.
Q2 — Habit: I drum on everything. Not loudly — quietly, with my fingertips. On my thigh in meetings, on the steering wheel at red lights, on the underside of restaurant tables. The pattern is almost never a song. It's usually whatever conversation I'm having translated into beats. People who are talking fast become quick patterns; pauses become rests. I once tried to stop and I couldn't — it's how I listen. If I'm not tapping, I'm not absorbing what you're saying. My partner has stopped asking me to stop.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain how a crowd suddenly turns from polite to enthusiastic at a wedding or a party. There's a specific moment, usually about forty minutes in, where the conversational hum reaches a certain density — enough overlapping voices that no single conversation is distinct anymore. The room stops sounding like individuals and starts sounding like one collective body. Once that happens, people start laughing louder, dancing happens, the energy turns. It's almost entirely about the texture of sound in the room reaching a critical mass. The crowd has tipped from group of strangers into something singular.
Q4 — Conflict: My ex-partner and I argued for years about the volume of music in our shared car. He wanted it as background; I wanted it audible. The real story underneath was that he heard music as a kind of pleasant wallpaper, and I heard it as something happening — like a person in the back seat. Asking me to turn it down was, to me, asking me to ignore someone in the room. Asking him to turn it up was, to him, demanding he sit next to a screaming child. We never resolved this. We broke up for unrelated reasons but I sometimes think we never could have stayed together.
Q5 — Sound: The exact cadence of my father's footsteps coming up the stairs. Always the same — three quick steps, a longer one on the fourth, a small pause on the landing. I knew it was him from anywhere in the apartment. The pattern was so distinct that when I was older and he was sick, I could tell from his footsteps when he was having a bad day. His rhythm slowed by maybe a quarter of a second per step on those days. I never told him I tracked this. It would have made him self-conscious about something he didn't choose.
Q6 — Body: I was at a friend's recital and the cellist made a tiny mistake about three minutes in — a slightly bent note. My shoulders flinched before my mind heard it. I noticed afterward that my body had registered the wrong-note as a small physical pinch, like someone had tapped my collarbone. The rest of the audience hadn't moved. I think most people heard the music as a flow; I heard it as a series of specific events, each one with its own physical signature in me.
Q7 — Unworded: The way a really good song you've never heard before can feel familiar in the first ten seconds. Not because you've heard the melody — you haven't. But the song is moving in a pattern your nervous system already knew was possible. It's like recognizing a face you've never met. I've tried to explain this to people who don't have it, and I usually fail. It's not nostalgia. It's something closer to homecoming for a place you've never been.
